pattern: Reduce a two-group partition-gap question to sorted-neighbor scanning: the tightest possible gap between one group's max and the other's min is always achieved by some pair of adjacent elements once everything is sorted.
hints: Forget about which specific elements go in which group for a moment and think about what sorting the whole array reveals about how close two groups' boundary elements could ever be.

Any valid split places some prefix-like boundary between the two groups once the values are sorted; the partition value can never beat the smallest gap between two values that end up consecutive in sorted order.
Sort the array once and return the minimum difference between every pair of adjacent elements — that gap is always achievable and always optimal.

edge_inputs.description: two elements far apart, forced single split
unsorted input requiring an internal sort
minimum length with both elements at the maximum value
values spanning the extremes of the allowed range
one far outlier should not affect the true minimum gap among the rest
duplicate values create a zero-gap opportunity in the middle

stress.description: large random array at full scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, size)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1_000_000_000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]

stress.description: large array with many duplicate values, stresses zero-gap handling
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, size)
    pool = [rng.randint(1, 1_000_000_000) for _ in range(max(1, n // 100))]
    nums = [pool[rng.randint(0, len(pool) - 1)] for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
